1→# Makefile for libm accuracy test — A/B comparison
     2→# Target: RV32IM soft-float (Andes optimized assembly)
     3→#
     4→# Usage:
     5→#   make size           — report .text size for each assembly function
     6→#   make test-all       — build, run opt vs ref in QEMU, diff outputs
     7→#   make test-sinf      — test a single function
     8→
     9→TOOLCHAIN = /local/nick/SW_Release/build-ast542/build-toolchain/linux/nds32le-elf-mculib-v5
    10→PREFIX    = $(TOOLCHAIN)/bin/riscv32-elf-
    11→
    12→CC  = $(PREFIX)gcc
    13→AS  = $(PREFIX)gcc
    14→LD  = $(PREFIX)gcc
    15→
    16→ARCH    = -march=rv32imc -mabi=ilp32
    17→CFLAGS  = $(ARCH) -O2 -mvh -Wall
    18→ASFLAGS = $(ARCH)
    19→LDFLAGS = $(ARCH) -mvh -lm
    20→
    21→QEMU = /local/nick/qemu_v5/build/qemu-system-riscv32
    22→QEMU_FLAGS = -cpu andes-n25 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -semihosting -bios
    23→
    24→SIZE = $(PREFIX)size
    25→NM   = $(PREFIX)nm
    26→
    27→# Assembly sources
    28→ASM_SRCS = sf_atan.s sf_ceil.s sf_cos.s sf_fabs.s sf_floor.s sf_frexp.s \
    29→           sf_ldexp.s sf_modf.s sf_sin.s sf_tan.s sf_tanh.s \
    30→           sf_acos.s sf_asin.s sf_cosh.s sf_exp.s sf_fmod.s sf_log.s \
    31→           sf_log10.s sf_pow.s sf_sinh.s sf_sqrt.s \
    32→           sf_fpclassify.s
    33→ASM_OBJS = $(ASM_SRCS:.s=.o)
    34→
    35→# Note: sf_sincos.c and sf_scalbn.c need picolibc headers, excluded from
    36→# standalone size target. Measure those via full meson build.
    37→
    38→# Functions to test (must have test_<name>.c and sf_<name>.s)
    39→FUNCS = sinf cosf tanf atanf fabsf ceilf floorf expf sinhf coshf tanhf \
    40→        logf log10f sqrtf asinf acosf fmodf powf frexpf ldexpf modff \
    41→        fpclassifyf
    42→
    43→# Test output directory
    44→TESTDIR = test
    45→
    46→# Verbose build output (set V=1 to see brief build progress)
    47→V ?= 0
    48→ifeq ($(V),1)
    49→  Q =
    50→  MSG_AS  = @printf "  AS\t%s\n" $@;
    51→  MSG_CC  = @printf "  CC\t%s\n" $@;
    52→  MSG_LD  = @printf "  LD\t%s\n" $@;
    53→else
    54→  Q = @
    55→  MSG_AS =
    56→  MSG_CC =
    57→  MSG_LD =
    58→endif
    59→
    60→# --- Pattern rules ---
    61→
    62→%.o: %.s
    63→	$(MSG_AS)$(AS) $(ASFLAGS) -c -o $@ $<
    64→
    65→$(TESTDIR)/%.o: $(TESTDIR)/%.c
    66→	$(MSG_CC)$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c -o $@ $<
    67→
    68→# For each function, build opt (with asm) and ref (toolchain only) ELFs.
    69→# The asm object name: strip trailing 'f' from func name to get sf_<base>.o
    70→# e.g. sinf -> sf_sin.o, log10f -> sf_log10.o, fpclassifyf -> sf_fpclassify.o
    71→define FUNC_RULES
    72→$(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_opt.elf: $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1).o sf_$(patsubst %f,%,$(1)).o
    73→	$$(MSG_LD)$$(LD) -o $$@ $$^ $$(LDFLAGS)
    74→
    75→$(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_ref.elf: $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1).o
    76→	$$(MSG_LD)$$(LD) -o $$@ $$^ $$(LDFLAGS)
    77→
    78→test-$(1): $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_opt.elf $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_ref.elf
    79→	@$$(QEMU) $$(QEMU_FLAGS) $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_ref.elf > $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_ref.txt
    80→	@$$(QEMU) $$(QEMU_FLAGS) $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_opt.elf > $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_opt.txt
    81→	@if diff -q $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_ref.txt $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_opt.txt > /dev/null 2>&1; then \
    82→		printf "  %-14s PASS\n" $(1); \
    83→	else \
    84→		printf "  %-14s FAIL (%d diffs)\n" $(1) \
    85→			$$$$(diff $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_ref.txt $(TESTDIR)/test_$(1)_opt.txt | grep -c '^[<>]'); \
    86→	fi
    87→endef
    88→
    89→$(foreach f,$(FUNCS),$(eval $(call FUNC_RULES,$(f))))
    90→
    91→# --- Top-level targets ---
    92→
    93→OPT_ELFS = $(foreach f,$(FUNCS),$(TESTDIR)/test_$(f)_opt.elf)
    94→REF_ELFS = $(foreach f,$(FUNCS),$(TESTDIR)/test_$(f)_ref.elf)
    95→
    96→all: $(OPT_ELFS) $(REF_ELFS)
    97→
    98→test-all: $(foreach f,$(FUNCS),test-$(f))
    99→	@echo "Done."
   100→
   101→size: $(ASM_OBJS)
   102→	@echo "=== .text size (bytes) ==="
   103→	@$(foreach o,$(ASM_OBJS), \
   104→		printf "  %-20s %d\n" $(basename $(o)) \
   105→			$$($(SIZE) -A $(o) | awk '/\.text/ {print $$2}');)
   106→	@echo "  --------------------"
   107→	@printf "  %-20s %d\n" "TOTAL" \
   108→		$$($(SIZE) -A $(ASM_OBJS) | awk '/\.text/ {s+=$$2} END {print s}')
   109→
   110→list:
   111→	@echo "Targets: all size test-all clean list"
   112→	@echo "Per-function: $(foreach f,$(FUNCS),test-$(f))"
   113→
   114→clean:
   115→	rm -f $(ASM_OBJS) $(TESTDIR)/*.o $(TESTDIR)/*.elf $(TESTDIR)/*_ref.txt $(TESTDIR)/*_opt.txt
   116→
   117→.PHONY: all size test-all clean list $(foreach f,$(FUNCS),test-$(f))
   118→

1→# CLAUDE.md
     2→
     3→This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.
     4→
     5→## What This Directory Is
     6→
     7→RISC-V architecture-specific math library (`libm`) optimizations for picolibc. These replace generic C implementations from `libm/common/` and `libm/math/` with hand-tuned assembly or hardware-FPU-aware C code.
     8→
     9→See `README.md` in this directory for detailed function listings and build configuration matrix.
    10→
    11→## Build System
    12→
    13→This directory is built as part of the top-level picolibc Meson build. There is no standalone build. The `meson.build` here populates `srcs_libm_machine` which gets compiled into the libm static library.
    14→
    15→A separate `CMakeLists.txt` exists for CMake-based builds but only includes a minimal subset (C files, no assembly, no configuration branching).
    16→
    17→### Key build flags (set in top-level `meson.build`, consumed here)
    18→
    19→- `is_softfp` — software floating-point ABI (no hardware FPU)
    20→- `is_double_abi` — hardware double-precision FPU
    21→- `is_riscv64` — 64-bit RISC-V
    22→- `is_rvm` — M-extension (hardware multiply/divide)
    23→- `is_rv32e` — RV32E reduced 16-register ISA
    24→
    25→### Per-target library quirk
    26→
    27→For soft-float RV32 configs (both standard and RV32E), `sf_sincos.c` is compiled as a separate per-target `static_library` via the `foreach params : targets` loop, not added to `srcs_libm_machine`. This is because it needs per-multilib `c_args`. The resulting `lib_machine<target>` libraries are linked via `link_whole` elsewhere.
    28→
    29→## Assembly Conventions
    30→
    31→The `.s` files were originally written for the Andes NDS32 ISA and transliterated to RISC-V. Comments preserve the original NDS32 mnemonics (e.g., `# push $lp`, `# swi $r0, [$sp + #4]`).
    32→
    33→### Register mapping pattern
    34→
    35→Assembly uses standard RISC-V register names (`x10`–`x20`, `sp`, `ra`, `t6`). The NDS32-to-RISC-V register mapping is:
    36→- `$r0`–`$r5` → `x10`–`x15` (argument/return registers `a0`–`a5`)
    37→- `$r6`–`$r9` → `x9`, `x18`–`x20` (saved registers)
    38→- `$lp` → `ra` (return address)
    39→- `$ta` → `t6` (temporary)
    40→
    41→### Internal call graph
    42→
    43→Assembly math functions call shared Andes helper routines (`andes_*.s`). Key dependency chains:
    44→- Trig functions → `__gf_dpreduct`/`__gf_fpreduct` (argument reduction) → `__gf_exmul`/`__gf_exadd` (extended precision) → polynomial evaluators (`__gf_p4`–`__gf_p8`)
    45→- `exp`/`log`/`pow` → `__gf_dpexexp`/`__gf_dpexlog` → extended precision helpers
    46→- All assembly shares coefficient tables: `__gf_dpsincon`, `__gf_dpcoscon`, `__gf_fpsincof`, `__gf_fpcoscof`, etc.
    47→
    48→### RV32E variants
    49→
    50→`rv32e/` contains the same routines rewritten to use only `x0`–`x15` (16 registers). When modifying an assembly routine, the corresponding `rv32e/` version must also be updated.
    51→
    52→## Hard-float C Code (`hardfloat/`)
    53→
    54→Optimized C implementations that exploit hardware FPU instructions. Originally from ARM's Optimized Routines project (Apache 2.0 licensed).
    55→
    56→- Uses `#pragma GCC optimize ("O3")` for aggressive optimization
    57→- `ref_rredf.c`/`ref_rredf.h` provides the shared float argument reduction helper (`ref__mathlib_rredf`)
    58→- Polynomial coefficients are stored in global arrays (not `static const`) to control memory layout for performance
    59→
    60→## Standalone Test Builds (outside Meson)
    61→
    62→For quick accuracy/correctness testing of individual assembly routines, use the standalone `Makefile` in this directory. It compiles test C files and links them against the relevant assembly objects directly.
    63→
    64→### Toolchain
    65→
    66→Use the Andes `nds32le-elf-mculib-v5` toolchain for RV32 soft-float builds:
    67→
    68→```
    69→/local/nick/SW_Release/build-ast542/build-toolchain/linux/nds32le-elf-mculib-v5/bin/riscv32-elf-gcc
    70→```
    71→
    72→- GCC 14.2.0, bare-metal (`riscv32-elf`)
    73→- Ships with newlib (provides printf, math reference functions, etc.)
    74→- `-mvh` flag enables Andes virtual hosting (semihosting) for I/O via QEMU/GDB
    75→- Target flags: `-march=rv32im -mabi=ilp32` for soft-float M-extension builds
    76→
    77→### QEMU
    78→
    79→Use the Andes QEMU v5 build for running RV32 test ELFs:
    80→
    81→```
    82→/local/nick/qemu_v5/build/qemu-system-riscv32
    83→```
    84→
    85→- QEMU 9.2.0 (Andes fork, v9.2.0-401-g4581fc99a4)
    86→- Uses the `qemu-runner` agent type in Claude Code to handle semihosting output capture
    87→
    88→### Example: build and run sinf test
    89→
    90→```sh
    91→make clean && make          # builds both opt and ref ELFs
    92→make compare                # builds, runs both in QEMU, diffs outputs
    93→```
    94→
    95→The Makefile builds two ELFs: `test_sinf_opt.elf` (linked with Andes assembly) and `test_sinf_ref.elf` (linked with toolchain's libm only). The `compare` target runs both through QEMU and diffs the output.
    96→
    97→### Unconditionally compiled sources
    98→
    99→Regardless of build configuration, the following C files are always included: `s_copysign`, `s_finite`, `s_fma`, `s_isinf`, `s_isnan`, `s_llrint`, `s_llround`, and their `sf_` (float) counterparts plus `sf_fmax`, `sf_fmin`, `sf_lrint`, `sf_lround`.
   100→
   101→## Naming Conventions
   102→
   103→- `e_` — IEEE error-handling wrapper functions (e.g., `e_sqrt` wraps the core sqrt)
   104→- `s_` — double-precision standard functions
   105→- `sf_` — single-precision (float) standard functions
   106→- `k_` — kernel helpers (internal, e.g., `k_sin`, `k_cos`)
   107→- `er_` — reentrant variants (e.g., `er_lgamma`)
   108→- `andes_` — shared assembly building blocks (not standalone math functions)
   109→

1→#!/bin/bash
     2→# commit-size: Commit code changes with size metrics from 'make size'
     3→# Usage: commit-size "commit message"
     4→
     5→set -e
     6→
     7→if [ $# -eq 0 ]; then
     8→    echo "Usage: commit-size \"commit message\""
     9→    exit 1
    10→fi
    11→
    12→COMMIT_MSG="$1"
    13→SIZE_FILE="code_size_opt"
    14→
    15→echo "Running 'make size' to capture metrics..."
    16→make clean > /dev/null 2>&1
    17→METRICS=$(make size 2>&1)
    18→
    19→# Extract the TOTAL line and timestamp
    20→TOTAL=$(echo "$METRICS" | awk '/^  TOTAL/ {print $NF}')
    21→TIMESTAMP=$(date +"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
    22→
    23→if [ -z "$TOTAL" ]; then
    24→    echo "Error: Could not extract size metrics from 'make size'"
    25→    exit 1
    26→fi
    27→
    28→echo "Metrics captured: $TOTAL bytes"
    29→
    30→# Update the size tracking file
    31→{
    32→    echo "$METRICS" | grep -A 50 "^=== .text"
    33→} > "$SIZE_FILE"
    34→
    35→echo ""
    36→echo "Updated $SIZE_FILE"
    37→echo ""
    38→
    39→# Stage both code changes and size file (force add if in gitignore)
    40→git add -f "$SIZE_FILE"
    41→
    42→# Commit with original message + size note
    43→EXTENDED_MSG="$COMMIT_MSG
    44→
    45→Size metric: $TOTAL bytes total .text (via 'make size')
    46→
    47→Co-Authored-By: Claude Haiku 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>"
    48→
    49→git commit -m "$EXTENDED_MSG"
    50→
    51→echo ""
    52→echo "✓ Commit complete with size metrics"
    53→

### 5. Assembly Structure Observations and Optimization Opportunities

**General structure:**

Every function follows the same pattern:
1. Stack frame setup: `addi sp, sp, -N` + `sw ra, ...` + `sw x9/x18/x19/x20, ...`
2. Function body with calls to `__gf_fp*` helpers
3. Stack frame teardown: `lw` of all saved registers + `addi sp, sp, N` + `ret`

**Key observations:**

**Stack frame sizes are often larger than necessary.** For example:
- `frexpf` and `ldexpf` allocate 32-byte frames but also save 4 callee-saved regs (x9, x18, x19, x20) at offsets 0–12, yet `ra` is at offset 16. This wastes the upper 12 bytes (offsets 20–31 unused in `frexpf`). The allocated frame could potentially be consolidated.
- `sf_log.s` allocates a 32-byte frame but only saves `ra` (at offset 16) — the upper and lower halves are both sparsely used. It also does a secondary ad-hoc `pushm/popm` pattern (pushing x10–x15 onto the stack with manual `addi sp, sp, -32 / sw / lw / addi sp, sp, 32`) to save temporaries around a `__clzsi2` call, which uses extra instructions.

**`slli` + `srli` pairs (fabs pattern):** `sf_fabs.s` uses `slli x10, x10, 1` then `srli x10, x10, 1` (2 instructions = 8 bytes) to clear the sign bit. The recently added `sf_fabs.c` C version uses inline `fabs.s` for hardware-FPU targets, but the `.s` file (soft-float path) still uses the 2-instruction form. This was already optimized to 8 bytes in `code_size_opt` vs. 16 bytes in `code_size_ref` — confirming this was a prior win.

**`mv` instruction pairs around `mulhu/mul`:** In `sf_sin.s` and `sf_cos.s`, the pattern:
```asm
mv x20, x10          # save x10
mul x10, x13, x11   # mulr64 low half
mulhu x11, x13, x11 # mulr64 high half into x11 — but clobbers x10
mv x10, x20          # restore x10
```
These `mv` save/restore pairs exist because the NDS32 `mulr64` instruction wrote a 64-bit result into a pair (low, high) without needing a save/restore, but RISC-V `mul`/`mulhu` each read both source regs. The pattern appears at least 3 times in `sf_sin.s` and `sf_cos.s`. Register allocation could potentially eliminate these save/restore `mv`s, but it requires careful analysis of liveness across the sequence.

**Repeated `li x15, 2147483648` and similar large immediates:** Many functions load `0x80000000` (sign mask) into a register with `li x15, 2147483648`, which assembles as a 2-instruction `lui` + `addi`. These are unavoidable without a shared constant pool but appear multiple times within the same function (e.g., `sf_sqrt.s` does not repeat this, but `sf_log.s`, `sf_modf.s`, and `sf_ldexp.s` each load it once per function — acceptable).

**`ceil`/`floor` have an unused `sw ra` / stack frame:** `sf_ceil.s` and `sf_floor.s` both allocate a 16-byte stack frame and save `ra`, but they do not call any other functions (they contain only `tail` branches and returns). The `ra` save is unnecessary for leaf-like functions with only tail calls. Removing the stack frame (4 instructions saved = 16 bytes) would reduce `ceilf` from 144 bytes and `floorf` from 156 bytes. This is the same class of optimization as the already-applied `sf_fabs.c` leaf-function optimization noted in commit `cf0c4b6ea`.

**`sf_fpclassify.s` is a true leaf function** (multiple `ret` paths, no calls, no stack) — already optimal at 122 bytes.

**`sf_atan.s` is small and structured well** (88 bytes): allocates a 16-byte frame to save `x9` and `ra`, calls `__gf_fpexatan`, restores and returns. The frame is the minimum needed for one callee-saved reg + ra.

**`sf_exp.s` has no callee-saved registers** beyond `ra` in a 32-byte frame with only offset 16 used for `ra` and offset 4 used for a temporary. The 32-byte allocation wastes 24 bytes of stack space vs. a 8-byte frame (just `ra` + temp at offsets 0 and 4). This is a stack-frame tightening opportunity (saves no code bytes but reduces stack depth, relevant for deeply embedded targets).

**`sf_frexp.s` allocates 32 bytes but saves x9, x18, x19, x20 (4 regs)** even though inspection shows x18/x19/x20 are never actually used in the fast path — the sub-frame `pushm/popm` around `__clzsi2` is the real usage point, but those pushes use `x10–x13/x15` (caller-saved), not the outer frame's callee-saved regs. The outer frame's x18/x19/x20 saves may be vestigial from the NDS32 transliteration.
